title: Reproducibility rules in economics
text: In this two-part workshop, The first session covers foundational practices such as data management, version control, code organization, and documentation standards aligned with leading journals, helping students structure empirical workflows that enhance credibility and long-term impact. The second session builds on these foundations with hands-on applications and more advanced workflow strategies. Topics include preparing replication packages, improving computational efficiency, collaborative coding practices, and meeting journal data requirements. Together, the sessions provide practical tools to help students ensure their research meets evolving standards in applied economics.

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Economics
text:
Reproducibility vs Replicability (@Cacioppo.etal2015)






Definitions: Reproducibility



A research study is reproducible if anybody (including the author of the study) can generate exactly the same results by using the same materials (e.g., data, code, and documentation) and procedures used in the study.








Definitions: Replicability



A research study is replicable if other teams reach the same conclusion by applying the same procedure to the difference materials (e.g., data).


We will focus on reproducibility in this workshop.

Reproducibility vs Replicability
Here are the definitions of reproducibility and replicability from @Cacioppo.etal2015:






Definitions: Reproducibility



Reproducibility refers to the ability of a researcher to duplicate the results of a prior study using the same materials as were used by the original investigator.








Definitions: Replicability



A research study is replicable if other teams reach the same conclusion by applying the same procedure to the difference materials (e.g., data).

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Economics
section: How can we achieve reproducibility in our research?
text:
How can we achieve reproducibility in our research?






Rule



Every single action taken during the entire research process is documented in a way that anybody can follow to implement the same actions (no hidden actions) to produce exactly the same results.

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Economics
text:
Characteristics of high-quality reproducible research

Organized Project Folder
A well-structured project folder with clear organization of data, code, and documentation files makes it easier for others to navigate and understand the research workflow.
Streamlined Code
Workflows are automated through clearly annotated programs, making it easier for others to understand the replication process and improving the transparency of the research.
Comprehensive Documentation
Documentation of the data sources, methods, and reproduction steps saves time and enhances clarity for others who want to reproduce the research.

Reproducibility benefits you and your team members as well as the scientific community.

A project can be considered reproducible if the minimum requirements are met. However, it is still low-quality if reproducing it requires excessive time or resources. A high-quality reproducible project, on the other hand, demonstrates the following characteristics:

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
text:
Reproducibility and Replicability
Here are the definitions of reproducibility and replicability from Cacioppo et al. (2015):



Definitions: Reproducibility


“Reproducibility refers to the ability of a researcher to duplicate the results of a prior study using the same materials as were used by the original investigator.”







Definitions: Replicability


A research study is replicable if other teams reach the same conclusion by applying the same procedure to the difference materials (e.g., data).




In essence, reproducibility asks:
“Can another researcher go from raw data to final results using only the materials you provide?”

So, what should we do to make our research reproducible?


Reproducing research involves using the original data and code, while replicating research involves new data collection and similar methods used in previous studies, the report says. Even when a study was rigorously conducted according to best practices, correctly analyzed, and transparently reported, it may fail to be replicated. (https://phys.org/news/2019-05-replicability-science.html)

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research
section: How can we achieve reproducibility in our research?
text:
How can we achieve reproducibility in our research?



Minimum Rule


Every single action taken during the entire research process is documented in a way that anybody can follow to implement the same actions (no hidden actions) to produce exactly the same results.




The following characteristics can further enhance the quality of reproducible research:


(i) Organized Project Folder


(ii) Streamlined Code 

(iii) Comprehensive Documentation 



Even if a project meets the minimum requirements for reproducibility, it may still be considered low-quality if reproducing it requires excessive time or resources. On the other hand, a high-quality reproducible project demonstrates the following characteristics

Organized Project Folder
A well-structured project folder with clear organization of data, code, and documentation files makes it easier for others to navigate and understand the research workflow.
Streamlined Code

Workflows are automated through clearly annotated programs, making it easier for others to understand the replication process and improving the transparency of the research.
less manual edits, copy-past steps, ad hoc file handling, etc. that can be error-prone and time-consuming.

Comprehensive Documentation

Documentation of the data sources, methods, and reproduction steps saves time and enhances clarity for others who want to reproduce the research.
The documentation should provide setup instructions, dependencies, and execution order.

title: Day 1: Introduction to Reproducibility in Scientific Research

Key Components of Reproducible Research



Minimum Rule


Every single action taken during the entire research process is documented in a way that anybody can follow to implement the same actions (no hidden actions) to produce exactly the same results.




The following characteristics can further enhance the quality of reproducible research:


(i) Organized Project Folder


(ii) Streamlined Code 

(iii) Comprehensive Documentation 



Even if a project meets the minimum requirements for reproducibility, it may still be considered low-quality if reproducing it requires excessive time or resources. On the other hand, a high-quality reproducible project demonstrates the following characteristics

Organized Project Folder
A well-structured project folder with clear organization of data, code, and documentation files makes it easier for others to navigate and understand the research workflow.
Streamlined Code

Workflows are automated through clearly annotated programs, making it easier for others to understand the replication process and improving the transparency of the research.
less manual edits, copy-past steps, ad hoc file handling, etc. that can be error-prone and time-consuming.

Comprehensive Documentation

Documentation of the data sources, methods, and reproduction steps saves time and enhances clarity for others who want to reproduce the research.
The documentation should provide setup instructions, dependencies, and execution order.

What is Reproducibility?
